html:
Romeo and Juliet — 10 Most Likely AQA Questions + Skeleton Essay Plans

For: AQA GCSE English Literature, Paper 1 Section A (Shakespeare). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). ~50 minutes.

Source. The plans below distil the ideas, frames and analytical moves from Sophicly's 9 Grade-9 Romeo and Juliet model answers (`Model Answers/AQA/Romeo and Juliet/`). Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes, the same controlling concept, and the same reading-strategies students have already learned in the Romeo and Juliet crib sheet.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

1. The AQA-style question.

2. The full typical extract in a blockquote so the student knows what scene to expect.

3. The frame line (the controlling argument behind the plan).

4. A short technique-term gloss block so every term in the plan is translated into plain English.

5. A skeleton plan in the locked structure used across every Sophicly worked essay:

• Introduction — 3 sentences (~110 words): Hook + Building sentence + Thesis.

• Body Paragraph 1 — 7 sentences (Beginning of the play; ~210–225 words).

• Body Paragraph 2 — 7 sentences (Middle of the play; the printed extract often sits here; ~225–250 words).

• Body Paragraph 3 — 7 sentences (End of the play; ~225–250 words).

• Conclusion — 4 sentences (~110–155 words).

Total ~1000 words on the page in the exam.

The seven-sentence body-paragraph shape (memorise this):

1. Topic — pure concept, no techniques named yet.

2. Technique + evidence — name the technique and quote the line.

3. Close analysis — the words inside the quote, unpacked.

4. Effect on audience 1 — what the audience feels or thinks.

5. Effect on audience 2 — a different feeling or thought.

6. Author's purpose — what Shakespeare may be arguing.

7. Context (AO3) — one factor, woven in, never bolted on.

Whole-essay cohesion (Sophicly standard). Every step of the plan must form a single logical chain. BP2's topic sentence opens with "Where Act X had installed Y, Act Z extends/escalates/converts that into…"; BP3's topic sentence opens with "Where Act Z had Y, Act V names/destroys/redeems it into…"; the conclusion's restated thesis retraces the chain using the same key nouns. The three BPs operate at three different LEVELS of the same diagnosis.

Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes — the Romeo and Juliet corpus rests on Act 1 Scene 1 (the brawl + the Prince's intervention), Act 1 Scene 2 (Paris's suit + Capulet's consultative voice), Act 1 Scene 4 (Queen Mab + Romeo's "my mind misgives"), Act 1 Scene 5 (the feast + sonnet-shared first sight + Tybalt's challenge), Act 2 Scene 2 (the balcony), Act 2 Scene 3 (Friar Lawrence's pharmacological soliloquy), Act 3 Scene 1 (Mercutio's death + Tybalt's death + "A plague o' both your houses"), Act 3 Scene 5 (Capulet's "young baggage" + Juliet's "chopt logic" defiance), Act 4 Scene 1 (the potion plan), Act 5 Scene 2 (the failed letter to Mantua), and Act 5 Scene 3 (the tomb). Memorise these scenes once, with two embedded quotations per scene, and every AQA question becomes a recombination of the same corpus.

---

Memorise these once

Controlling concept of Romeo and Juliet. Verona's honour-culture and dynastic-marriage system make the deaths of the young inevitable from Act 1 Scene 1 onward; the play's catastrophe is not contingent on bad luck (the lost letter, the late awakening) but structurally produced by the social order the lovers have been trying to escape. Shakespeare stages adolescent love as the moral critique of the very order that destroys it.

Six key terms that recur in the plans below — gloss every one when you use it.

• Honour-culture = the Elizabethan social system in which a man's reputation in the community was the highest moral asset, ratified through public acts (oaths, duels, satisfactions); Tybalt embodies this system at its most violent.

• Dynastic marriage = elite marriage negotiated for land, lineage and political alliance; daughters were treated as transferable assets whose value was guaranteed by the family's honour. Capulet's arrangement of Juliet to Paris dramatises the system at full extension.

• Hamartia = a fatal error in action (something the protagonist does), NOT a fatal flaw of character. Romeo's hamartia is the killing of Tybalt in Act 3 Scene 1 — the act that exiles him and sets up the catastrophe.

• Petrarchan convention = the love-poetry tradition descended from the fourteenth-century Italian poet Petrarch, supplying conventional oxymorons (icy fire, sweet pain) and unattainable-mistress imagery; Romeo opens the play inside that tradition and the play measures the distance he travels out of it.

• Sonnet = a fourteen-line lyric poem in a fixed metre and rhyme scheme; Romeo and Juliet's first exchange in Act 1 Scene 5 is structured as a Shakespearean sonnet shared between two voices, signalling that the love is co-authored from the first line.

• Pathetic fallacy = the natural world reflecting human emotion; the dawn that ends the lovers' wedding-night, the moonlight of the balcony, and the tomb's "presence" of darkness are the play's pathetic-fallacy moments.

Always gloss the term in brackets the first time you use it in an essay. AQA examiners reward the term + the gloss together.

---

Q1 — Romeo's character

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shakespeare presents Romeo's character in Romeo and Juliet.

>

Write about:

>

- how Shakespeare presents Romeo in this extract

- how Shakespeare presents Romeo in the play as a whole.

>

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Typical Extract

ROMEO: But soft, what light through yonder window breaks?

It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.

Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon,

Who is already sick and pale with grief

That thou, her maid, art far more fair than she.

Be not her maid, since she is envious.

Her vestal livery is but sick and green,

And none but fools do wear it. Cast it off.

It is my lady. O, it is my love!

O, that she knew she were!

She speaks, yet she says nothing. What of that?

Her eye discourses; I will answer it.

I am too bold. 'Tis not to me she speaks.

Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven,

Having some business, do entreat her eyes

To twinkle in their spheres till they return.

What if her eyes were there, they in her head?

The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars

As daylight doth a lamp. Her eyes in heaven

Would through the airy region stream so bright

That birds would sing and think it were not night.

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Petrarchan convention = the love-poetry tradition supplying conventional oxymorons and unattainable-mistress imagery.

• Oxymoron = two contradictory terms held together ("loving hate", "feather of lead").

• Celestial / astral imagery = imagery drawn from the sun, moon, stars and heavens.

• Sonnet = the fourteen-line lyric form Romeo and Juliet's first exchange shares between two voices.

• Hamartia = a fatal error in action — Romeo's killing of Tybalt in Act 3 Scene 1.

• Pathetic fallacy = the natural world reflecting human emotion.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (poignant question): Can a young man hold three demands of honour at once — the perfect courtly lover, the fierce defender of his family's name, and the loyal son of his family's lineage — without destroying himself and the wider society around him?

• Building sentence (historical context): The cult of honour gripped early modern society at every level, demanding three perfections from the same young nobleman at once. Sir Thomas Elyot's The Boke Named the Governour (London, 1532) defined a man's "natural perfection" as "fierce, hardy, strong in opinion and covetous of glory" — the fierce defender of his family's reputation. At the same time, Castiglione's The Book of the Courtier (Italy, 1528; English translation 1561) added the perfect courtly lover, fluent in the language of devotion. Beneath both lay the demand of lineage — the loyal son protecting his family's name — and Renaissance tragedy stages what happens when all three demands of honour are held to their extreme inside the same young body.

• Thesis: With these contextual factors in mind, this essay will explore how Shakespeare presents Romeo as a young man asked to hold all three demands of honour at once. Romeo's voice arrives as the perfect courtly lover at the balcony, is forced into the fierce defender's voice in the duel after Mercutio's death, and ends in his death at the tomb where his family's lineage finally consumes him. The essay argues that no young man could hold the three demands of honour at their extreme — and the cost of that contradiction reaches not just Romeo but the wider society that demanded all three at once.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Act 2 Scene 2, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Act 2 Scene 2, the balcony — Romeo discovering real feeling for the first time)

• Topic: In the play's opening movement, Shakespeare stages Romeo's first voice as the perfect courtly lover — the demand of honour Castiglione's courtier-tradition placed on every young noble. Romeo's love for Rosaline had been borrowed performance, but his love for Juliet at the balcony arrives as the courtly lover's voice in its real form, the first of the three honour-demands made flesh.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "But soft, what light through yonder window breaks? / It is the east, and Juliet is the sun. / Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon" combines celestial metaphor (Juliet as the sun, the moon pushed aside as envious) with the imperative verb "kill" (Romeo's voice now giving orders to the heavens).

• Close analysis: The metaphor "Juliet is the sun" claims a cosmic identification rather than a borrowed comparison. The imperative "kill the envious moon" turns Romeo from a person asking for love into one ordering the heavens about — his voice now arranging the universe rather than asking it for favours.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the celestial scale and recognises Romeo arriving at his first true Petrarchan-lover voice — real worship discovered, not borrowed performance.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience catches the displacement of Diana, the moon-goddess of distance and reserve, and infers Romeo's love as one that overthrows the play's earlier divine order.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging Romeo's first true arrival as a Petrarchan lover — the same young man who had performed convention for Rosaline now reaching real worship for Juliet inside a single speech.

• Context (AO3): The plosive force of "kill," aimed at a divine moon-goddess, plants the destructive logic that will later govern the play. Brian Gibbons (Arden Shakespeare 2nd Series, 1980) argues that Romeo and Juliet's poetic vocabulary deliberately holds opposing terms together — light and dark, love and death — mirroring the contradiction Romeo's society forces on him.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Act 3 Scene 1, the city's killing pulls Romeo in — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 3 Scene 1, "O, I am fortune's fool!" after the Tybalt-killing — Romeo's discovery cut short by the city's violence)

• Topic: Where the play's opening movement had given Romeo the courtly lover's voice, Act 3 Scene 1 forces him into the second demand of honour — the fierce defender of his family's name, sword in hand against Tybalt. Elyot's "fierce, hardy" ideal cannot coexist with Castiglione's courtly lover: the courtly voice dies the moment the fierce-defender voice is required.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "O, I am fortune's fool!" combines apostrophe (Romeo addressing himself, almost in the third person) with the possessive-genitive metaphor "fortune's fool" (Romeo treating himself as Fortune's plaything), the same voice that had risen at the balcony now naming its own loss of control.

• Close analysis: The exclamation "O" echoes the "O" of Romeo's earlier love-poetry, but here it does the opposite job — it no longer hails the beloved but names the speaker as the object of forces beyond him. The phrase "fortune's fool" turns Romeo from a person who acts into a person being acted upon, the same voice now describing its own helplessness.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the echo and recognises Romeo's voice arriving at its second stage — the discovery of love broken by violence the same speaker has just committed.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience catches the word "fortune's" as the play's first admission that this catastrophe was always coming — Romeo finally putting words to the grip his society has on his own life.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging this killing as the moment Romeo's society forces its second value onto him — the Petrarchan lover of the balcony pulled into the honour-violence the same society also demands.

• Context (AO3): Susan Snyder's The Comic Matrix of Shakespeare's Tragedies (1979) reads Mercutio's death as the moment the play turns from comedy to tragedy — Romeo's "fortune's fool" the first time the play names that the comedy has collapsed into the killing the honour-system has been producing.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Act 5 Scene 3, the tomb — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 5 Scene 3, the tomb — Romeo's death as the only public space Verona ever built for his love)

• Topic: Where Act 3 Scene 1 had pulled Romeo into the fierce-defender's voice, Act 5 Scene 3 stages the third demand of honour consuming him — the loyal son whose family's lineage finally requires his body in the tomb beside his enemy's. The wider cost reaches beyond Romeo: Mercutio, Tybalt, Paris and Lady Montague all die before the cult of honour exhausts its claim.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Eyes, look your last! Arms, take your last embrace! and, lips, O you / The doors of breath, seal with a righteous kiss / A dateless bargain to engrossing death!" combines apostrophic address to body parts (eyes, arms, lips named one after another) with legal-commercial metaphor "dateless bargain" (the love-death framed as a contract that never runs out).

• Close analysis: The list of three body-parts named in turn lets the audience see Romeo taking visible stock of himself before he dies; the legal word "bargain" turns the union into a contract Verona's law had refused to recognise but death now finally signs.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the address to eyes, arms and lips and recognises Romeo's voice arriving at its final scale — the love-death the only place his society has left for the union.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience catches the legal word "bargain" and infers that the tomb completes the marriage Verona had refused, the "dateless bargain" the only contract Romeo's society will ever sign for him.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing Romeo's death as the cost of his society's antithesis — two opposing values, Petrarchan love and honour-violence, both forcing themselves onto the same young man at the same time.

• Context (AO3): Emma Smith's This Is Shakespeare (Pelican, 2019) argues that the deaths in Romeo and Juliet are produced by Verona's society rather than caused by bad luck. Romeo's tomb-voice is the love finally being recognised — but only by the one public space his society had left open for it.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, Romeo's voice opens as the perfect courtly lover at the balcony, is forced into the fierce defender of his family's name in the duel, and ends as the loyal son whose family lineage consumes him at the tomb. Three demands of honour the cult of early modern society held over the same young body at once — and the contradiction destroys not only Romeo but the wider society around him.

• Controlling concept: The play argues that the cult of honour, taken to its extreme in three demands at once, makes the deaths of the young inevitable. Romeo is the construct through which Shakespeare measures what those three demands of honour cost when held simultaneously inside the same young body.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing Romeo's death as the cost of early modern society's three-fold honour-demand — the perfect courtly lover, the fierce defender of the family's name, and the loyal son of the lineage. The contradiction destroys him, and through him, the wider society that demanded all three extremes at once.

• Ultimate moral: The play's final message is that no young man could hold the three demands of early modern honour at their extreme — perfect courtly lover, fierce defender of the family-name, and loyal son of the lineage — without destroying both himself and the wider society that demanded them. Romeo's death is the cost the contradiction always required, but the wider tragedy reaches into the deaths of Mercutio, Tybalt, Paris and Lady Montague — and into the two families finally forced to face what the cult of honour had cost them.

Q2 — Juliet's character

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shakespeare presents Juliet's character in Romeo and Juliet.

>

Write about:

>

- how Shakespeare presents Juliet in this extract

- how Shakespeare presents Juliet in the play as a whole.

>

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Typical Extract

Capulet: How how, how how, chopt logic? What is this?

"Proud," and "I thank you," and "I thank you not";

And yet "not proud," mistress minion, you,

Thank me no thankings, nor proud me no prouds,

But fettle your fine joints 'gainst Thursday next,

To go with Paris to Saint Peter's Church,

Or I will drag thee on a hurdle thither.

Out, you green-sickness carrion! Out, you baggage!

You tallow-face!

Lady Capulet: Fie, fie! What, are you mad?

Juliet: Good father, I beseech you on my knees,

Hear me with patience but to speak a word.

Capulet: Hang thee, young baggage, disobedient wretch!

I tell thee what: get thee to church o' Thursday,

Or never after look me in the face.

Speak not, reply not, do not answer me.

My fingers itch. Wife, we scarce thought us blest

That God had lent us but this only child;

But now I see this one is one too much,

And that we have a curse in having her:

Out on her, hilding!

Nurse: God in heaven bless her!

You are to blame, my lord, to rate her so.

Capulet: And why, my lady wisdom? hold your tongue,

Good prudence; smatter with your gossips, go.

Nurse: I speak no treason.

Capulet: O, God ye god-den.

Nurse: May not one speak?

Capulet: Peace, you mumbling fool!

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Dynastic marriage = elite marriage negotiated for land, lineage and political alliance.

• Stichomythia = rapid one-line exchanges between speakers, often signalling escalating conflict.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Imperative mood = grammatical command.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior (used sparingly in drama through aside).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (poignant question): Juliet says of Romeo: "My only love sprung from my only hate." What is the cost when a family defends the honour of its name to the point of destroying the innocent lives that carry the name?

• Building sentence (historical context): Shakespeare drew his story from Arthur Brooke's 1562 poem The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet. Brooke's preface defended the family-name by warning young people against defying their parents and "thralling themselves to unhonest desire." Shakespeare deleted Brooke's punishments — no apothecary hanged, no nurse banished — and replaced them with the golden statues: twin monuments paid for by reconciled fathers, equally honouring both lovers as moral examples rather than warnings. Where Brooke had defended the family-name by punishing the young, Shakespeare's tragedy stages what that name-honour costs when it is taken to its extreme — the destruction of the very children whose names it was supposed to protect.

• Thesis: With these contextual factors in mind, this essay will explore how Shakespeare presents Juliet as the construct through which his play measures the cost of name-honour taken to its extreme. Juliet's voice names the family-name as her enemy at the balcony, then meets her family's name-honour erupting back at her in the "chopt logic" confrontation, and ends in choosing death over the marriage her name demands. The essay argues that Juliet dies because her family had taken the honour of the Capulet name to such an extreme that her own moral discovery — "My only love sprung from my only hate" — could not be allowed to live.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Act 2 Scene 2, the balcony's "What's in a name?" — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Act 2 Scene 2, the balcony's "What's in a name?" — Juliet's consenting-person voice arrives)

• Topic: In the play's opening movement, Shakespeare stages Juliet as the lover who names the family-name itself as her enemy. The young woman who has just said "My only love sprung from my only hate" now reasons aloud at the balcony — naming the inherited Capulet name as the only thing keeping her from Romeo.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Juliet's "What's in a name? That which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet" combines rhetorical question (Juliet asking herself, and the audience, to think the problem through with her) with the rose-experiment (a controlled test in which the name is changed but the thing named stays the same).

• Close analysis: The rhetorical question opens space for Juliet to reason aloud about the family-name her culture has taught her to defend. The rose-experiment isolates that name — the family-honour she has inherited — as the only thing standing between her own moral judgement and Romeo, and proves, by the end of the line, that the name does not change the thing it names.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Juliet's reasoning and recognises her treating the family-name as a vocabulary problem — something her family has chosen to defend, not something the world has fixed.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience catches the philosophical scope — a thirteen-year-old reasoning her way out of a name-loyalty her elders cannot question — and recognises this as the play's first moment of love arrived at outside the name.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging Juliet's reasoning as the moment a young woman first names the family-name itself as the obstacle, in a culture so committed to defending its names that her thinking already counts as defiance.

• Context (AO3): René Weis (Arden Shakespeare 3rd Series, 2012) notes that the orchard scene gives Juliet more spoken lines than Romeo, the lovers reasoning their way to love together. The rhetorical balance registers the play's argument that this love is a moral discovery shared between equal voices, not a masculine pursuit Juliet receives.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Act 3 Scene 5, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 3 Scene 5, the "chopt logic" confrontation — society's two demands collide in the open)

• Topic: Where the play's opening movement had given Juliet her own moral reasoning, Act 3 Scene 5 stages her family's name-honour erupting back at her the moment her judgement breaks the surface. The Capulet household — which had performed honoured love — now reveals the violence the family-name had been holding in reserve.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Capulet's "How how, how how, chopt logic? What is this? … hang thee, young baggage, disobedient wretch!" combines dehumanising metaphor "young baggage" (Juliet reduced from honoured daughter to discarded property in a single word) with the imperative "hang thee" (the father's licensed violence threatened directly).

• Close analysis: The compound noun "baggage" compresses the honoured-daughter lexicon of Act 1 into discarded-property in a single word — the family-name producing the opposite emotional output the moment Juliet's own moral judgement contradicts it. The imperative "hang" names the violence the family's name-honour had quietly relied on all along.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the speed of the collapse from honoured daughter to disposable property, and recognises name-honour as a love that turns violent the moment the daughter's own moral agency steps outside the family-name.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Juliet hold her position under threatened disinheritance and infers her refusal as the moment the family's love and the family's violence are revealed as two faces of the same name.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging the "young baggage" collapse as the moment his play makes visible the cost of name-honour-extreme — a family so committed to defending its name that violence against the daughter is the only language left to it.

• Context (AO3): Refusal of an arranged elite match risked disinheritance, severance from the social network and reputational damage to the family. Capulet's threat sits inside legal and social precedent every Elizabethan audience recognised, which is precisely why Juliet's refusal lands as the play's deepest moment of agency.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Act 4 Scene 3, the potion soliloquy — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 4 Scene 3, the potion soliloquy — Juliet's solo agency at the play's most isolated moment)

• Topic: Where Act 3 Scene 5 had revealed the family-name as the source of its own violence, Act 4 Scene 3's potion soliloquy stages Juliet's solo answer. The moral agent now chooses death-likeness rather than the marriage her name demands — fulfilling alone the "My only love sprung from my only hate" she had named at first sight of Romeo.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Juliet's "My dismal scene I needs must act alone. / Come, vial. / What if this mixture do not work at all? / Shall I be married then to-morrow morning?" combines the isolated stage-direction declaration "must act alone" (Juliet narrating her own solitude) with rapid-fire conditional questioning "What if… what if…" (her own intellect testing the vial's risks).

• Close analysis: The phrase "I needs must act alone" names Juliet as her own moral agent at the play's most isolated moment — no nurse, no parents, no friar present. The conditional "What if" loop demonstrates her thinking through every contingency before choosing, the same moral reasoning she first used at the balcony now operating with no audience and no help.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience sees Juliet alone on stage and recognises this as the moment the play's young woman becomes her own moral judge — choosing fully, with full knowledge of what the family-name will exact for her refusal.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience hears the conditional questioning and infers that Juliet is not desperate but reasoning — the same moral agency she displayed at the balcony, now meeting the family-name at the only place it had left her: private, solitary, in the dark.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing Juliet's solo agency here as the play's deepest measure of name-honour-extreme — a young woman forced to think her clearest moral choice in private, because her family had built no public space inside the name for it.

• Context (AO3): Shakespeare's culture imagined death itself as a bridegroom: Capulet's later lament "Death is my son-in-law, death is my heir" (4.5.38–9) makes the link explicit. Juliet's solitary choice of the vial reaches for that figure of death-as-bridegroom because the Capulet name had left her no living one it could honour.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, Juliet's voice opens at the balcony naming the family-name as her enemy, is met by her family's name-honour erupting back in the "chopt logic" confrontation, and ends in the solitary choice of death over the marriage her name demands. Three stages of the same young woman destroyed by name-honour taken to its extreme — the cost the Capulet name paid in the body of its own daughter.

• Controlling concept: The play argues that name-honour, taken to its extreme, makes the deaths of the young inevitable; Juliet's tragedy is the play's clearest moment of a family so committed to defending its name that family love and family violence become one and the same.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing Juliet's tragedy as the play's deepest measure of name-honour-extreme. The deaths of both Juliet and Romeo are the cost the families could not see until their own children's bodies forced them to face it — the golden statues of Act 5 Scene 3 the belated recognition of the innocent lives that had carried the name all along.

• Ultimate moral: The play's final message is that name-honour, taken to its extreme, destroys the innocent lives that carry the name. Juliet and Romeo are the cost the families do not see until their own children's bodies force them to face it — and "My only love sprung from my only hate" names the contradiction the families could only resolve over their children's tombs.

Q3 — The theme of love

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shakespeare presents the theme of love in Romeo and Juliet.

>

Write about:

>

- how Shakespeare presents love in this extract

- how Shakespeare presents love in the play as a whole.

>

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Typical Extract

Romeo: O, she doth teach the torches to burn bright!

It seems she hangs upon the cheek of night

Like a rich jewel in an Ethiop's ear;

Beauty too rich for use, for earth too dear!

So reveals a snowy dove trooping with crows,

As yonder lady o'er her fellows reveals.

The measure done, I'll watch her place of stand,

And, touching hers, make blessed my rude hand.

Did my heart love till now? Forswear it, sight!

For I ne'er saw true beauty till this night.

>

Romeo: If I profane with my unworthiest hand

This holy shrine, the gentle sin is this:

My lips, two blushing pilgrims, ready stand

To smooth that rough touch with a tender kiss.

Juliet: Good pilgrim, you do wrong your hand too much,

Which mannerly devotion reveals in this;

For saints have hands that pilgrims' hands do touch,

And palm to palm is holy palmers' kiss.

Romeo: Have not saints lips, and holy palmers too?

Juliet: Ay, pilgrim, lips that they must use in prayer.

Romeo: O, then, dear saint, let lips do what hands do!

They pray; grant thou, lest faith turn to despair.

Juliet: Saints do not move, though grant for prayer's sake.

Romeo: Then move not while my prayer's effect I take.

[Kisses her]

Thus from my lips, by thine, my sin is purged.

Juliet: Then have my lips the sin that they have took.

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Petrarchan convention = the inherited European love-poetry tradition of oxymorons and unattainable mistresses.

• Sonnet = a fourteen-line lyric poem in fixed metre and rhyme; Romeo and Juliet's first exchange is structured as a Shakespearean sonnet shared between two voices.

• Celestial / astral imagery = imagery of sun, moon and stars.

• Pathetic fallacy = nature reflecting human emotion.

• Oxymoron = two contradictory terms held together ("happy dagger").

• Liebestod = German "love-death" — the literary tradition (medieval through Wagner) in which love completes itself in joint death.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (poignant question): Shakespeare opens Romeo and Juliet by calling its lovers "a pair of star-cross'd lovers." What does the play reveal about love itself when it arrives inside families so committed to defending the honour of their names that the children must die for finding it?

• Building sentence (historical context): Shakespeare drew his story from Arthur Brooke's 1562 poem The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet, whose preface defended the family-name by warning young people against defying their parents. Shakespeare deleted Brooke's punishments and replaced them with the golden statues of Act 5 Scene 3 — twin monuments paid for by reconciled fathers, equally honouring both lovers as moral examples rather than warnings. Where Brooke had defended the family-name by punishing the lovers, Shakespeare's tragedy stages what name-honour cannot accommodate: a love arrived at by mutual moral recognition between two equal voices.

• Thesis: With these contextual factors in mind, this essay will explore how Shakespeare presents love as the moral alternative the name-honour-extreme of Verona destroys. Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Apostrophe = direct address to someone absent or non-human.

• Pathetic fallacy = nature reflecting human emotion.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

Verona's families have built their whole society around the defence of their names — and Shakespeare's tragedy stages what that defence costs when it is taken to its extreme.

• Thesis: With these contextual factors in mind, this essay will explore how Shakespeare narrows violence inward across three stages, tracking the same name-honour-extreme as it consumes the bodies of the very children whose names it claimed to defend. Violence opens as public-civic disorder in the streets, narrows to kinsman-killing under Tybalt's invocation of "the stock and honour of my kin," and ends as self-violence at the tomb. The essay argues that the violence in Romeo and Juliet is name-honour-extreme narrowing inward — the only remaining object the bodies of Romeo and Juliet themselves.

She is the hopeful lady of my earth:

But woo her, gentle Paris, get her heart,

My will to her consent is but a part;

An she agree, within her scope of choice

Lies my consent and fair according voice.

This night I hold an old accustom'd feast,

Whereto I have invited many a guest,

Such as I love; and you, among the store,

One more, most welcome, makes my number more.

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Honour-culture = the Elizabethan social system in which a man's reputation in the community was the highest moral asset.

• Dynastic marriage = elite marriage negotiated for land, lineage and political alliance.

• Imperative declarative = a grammatically declarative sentence that functions as a command.

• Civic register = the vocabulary of public administration and law.

• Conditional clause = a clause beginning with "if" — language of imagined possibility, not commitment.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior (drama's closest equivalent is aside).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Elizabethan elite marriages were negotiated for land, lineage and political alliance, with the bride's consent legally required but socially overridden; English conduct literature of the 1590s — Vives's Instruction of a Christian Woman and Edmund Tilney's Flower of Friendship (1568) — praised consultative fathers while affirming the father's final right of veto.

• Building sentence (historical context): That contradiction was the dynastic-marriage system's central legal-social mechanism, and Capulet's swing from consultative voice in Act 1 Scene 2 to decision-mode in Act 3 Scene 4 to dehumanising rage in Act 3 Scene 5 dramatises the contradiction the conduct literature concealed. The civic surface and the dynastic mechanics belong to one system, and the system reveals its mechanics whenever the consultation produces refusal.

• Thesis: Across Act 1 Scene 2's "my will to her consent is but a part" consultative voice, Act 3 Scene 4's "she shall be married to this noble earl" announced before consultation, and Act 3 Scene 5's "young baggage, disobedient wretch" dehumanising rage, Shakespeare stages honour-culture and dynastic marriage at three levels — consultative civic surface, decision-mode, and dehumanising rage — three stages of the same possessive grammar producing different responses to the same daughter.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Act 1 Scene 2, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Act 1 Scene 2, Paris's suit — consultative voice)

• Topic: In the Act 1 Scene 2 negotiation, Shakespeare installs honour-culture and dynastic marriage at their first level — the consultative voice, Capulet's response to Paris's suit operating as the era's civic-rhetoric surface.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Capulet's "My child is yet a stranger in the world… My will to her consent is but a part" combines the conditional-temporal clause "yet a stranger" (Juliet's youth held as the deferring reason for delay) with the rhyming-couplet civic-political register (the formal-decorum verse-form for negotiated elite agreements).

• Close analysis: The conditional "yet" admits the possibility of a future change of mind that the system will not in fact wait for; the rhyming couplet's formal closure mirrors the institution's actual closure, the father's authority intact even while voiced as shared.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience tracks civic consultation and registers Capulet's voice as the system's idealised public surface.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience pities the "my will… is but a part" phrase and reads consultation as the system's permitted vocabulary while sensing the dynastic mechanism waiting beneath it.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging this consultative voice to expose the gap between honour-culture's civic rhetoric and its dynastic mechanics; what the language calls a part is the whole.

• Context (AO3): Edmund Tilney's Flower of Friendship (1568) and other Elizabethan conduct literature praised consultative fathers while affirming the father's right of veto; the play dramatises the contradiction the conduct literature concealed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Act 3 Scene 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 3 Scene 4, Capulet's "she shall be married" — decision-mode)

• Topic: Where Act 1 Scene 2 had installed the consultative voice, Act 3 Scene 4 converts the system to decision-mode — Capulet's "she shall be married to this noble earl" dropping the consultative register the moment dynastic pressure intensifies.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Capulet's "Sir Paris, I will make a desperate tender / Of my child's love. I think she will be ruled / In all respects by me; nay, more, I doubt it not… o' Thursday let it be — o' Thursday, tell her, / She shall be married to this noble earl" combines the imperative-declarative future tense "shall be married" (the announcement framed as fixed administrative fact) with the dynastic-political compound "noble earl" (the title naming the alliance's actual content).

• Close analysis: The future tense "shall be" removes any conditional element from the announcement; the noun phrase "noble earl" lands the marriage's actual content as title-and-alliance rather than affection.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience registers the disappearance of the consultative voice from Act 1 Scene 2 and recognises the speed of the system's mode-shift under stress.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience shudders at the bride's complete absence from the announcement of her own marriage and infers consultation as having always been provisional.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging the disappearance of the consultative voice to reveal that consultation was always provisional; when dynastic pressure intensifies (after Tybalt's death and the political capital of the Capulet–Paris match), the institution speaks plainly.

• Context (AO3): Elizabethan elite marriages were often announced before the bride was informed; Capulet's plain declaration matches historical practice, and the contemporary audience would have recognised the "shall be married" register as the dynastic system's actual operating mode behind the consultative surface.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Act 3 Scene 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 3 Scene 5, Capulet's "young baggage" — dehumanising rage)

• Topic: Where Act 3 Scene 4 had converted the system to decision-mode, Act 3 Scene 5 reveals its dehumanising rage — the disposable-property lexicon the system reaches for the moment the asset refuses to comply.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Capulet's "Hang thee, young baggage, disobedient wretch! / I tell thee what — get thee to church o' Thursday, / Or never after look me in the face" combines the dehumanising metaphor "young baggage" (Juliet reduced from "hopeful lady of my earth" to discarded property in a single word) with the imperative "hang thee" (the father's licensed paternal violence threatened directly).

• Close analysis: The compound noun "baggage" compresses the dynastic-asset lexicon of Act 1 into the disposable-property lexicon of Act 3 — the same possessive grammar producing two different responses to the same asset; the imperative "hang" names the system's enforcement mechanism in legal-violent register.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience sees the speed of the collapse from honorific-asset to dehumanising-rage and recognises the dynastic-marriage system's grammar as a constant whose performance is the variable.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers the threatened disinheritance and infers that the system's tenderness and its violence are not opposites but two outputs of the same possessive logic — ownership the constant, performance the variable.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be staging the "young baggage" collapse to demonstrate that honour-culture's affection for daughters is conditional on compliance; the system can only see the daughter as an asset, and the question is whether the asset complies.

• Context (AO3): Refusal of an arranged elite match risked disinheritance, severance from the social network, and reputational damage to the house; Capulet's threat sits inside legal and social precedent the contemporary audience recognised, and Coppélia Kahn's Man's Estate (1981) reads the scene as the era's clearest dramatisation of the dynastic-marriage system's grammar of ownership.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Act 1 Scene 2 had installed as consultative civic voice in "my will to her consent is but a part", Act 3 Scene 4 had converted to decision-mode in "she shall be married to this noble earl", and Act 3 Scene 5 had completed as dehumanising rage in "young baggage, disobedient wretch" — three stages of the same possessive grammar producing different responses to the same daughter across the play's middle acts.

• Controlling concept: The play argues that Verona's honour-culture and dynastic-marriage system make the deaths of the young inevitable; the system's grammar is constant, and the catastrophe arrives as the predictable output of an order that admits the bride only as a transferable asset.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing the dynastic-marriage system as the era's deepest critique of elite-marriage practice — the consultative civic surface concealing a decision-mode the order reveals whenever the asset refuses to comply.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that consultation was always provisional, that "young baggage" is the system's grammar revealing itself, and that the dynastic-marriage system produces the catastrophe it had announced through the same vocabulary that opened the play.

Q10 — Mercutio's character

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shakespeare presents Mercutio's character in Romeo and Juliet.

>

Write about:

>

- how Shakespeare presents Mercutio in this extract

- how Shakespeare presents Mercutio in the play as a whole.

>

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Typical Extract

MERCUTIO: O then, I see Queen Mab hath been with you.

She is the fairies' midwife, and she comes

In shape no bigger than an agate-stone

On the forefinger of an alderman,

Drawn with a team of little atomies

Over men's noses as they lie asleep.

Her wagon-spokes made of long spinners' legs,

The cover of the wings of grasshoppers,

Her traces of the smallest spider's web,

Her collars of the moonshine's watery beams,

Her whip of cricket's bone, the lash of film,

Her wagoner a small grey-coated gnat,

Not half so big as a round little worm

Pricked from the lazy finger of a maid.

Her chariot is an empty hazelnut,

Made by the joiner squirrel or old grub,

Time out o' mind the fairies' coachmakers.

And in this state she gallops night by night

Through lovers' brains, and then they dream of love;

O'er courtiers' knees, that dream on court'sies straight;

O'er lawyers' fingers, who straight dream on fees;

O'er ladies' lips, who straight on kisses dream,

Which oft the angry Mab with blisters plagues,

Because their breaths with sweetmeats tainted are.

Sometime she gallops o'er a courtier's nose,

And then dreams he of smelling out a suit;

And sometime comes she with a tithe-pig's tail

Tickling a parson's nose as 'a lies asleep,

Then dreams he of another benefice.

Sometime she driveth o'er a soldier's neck,

And then dreams he of cutting foreign throats,

Of breaches, ambuscadoes, Spanish blades,

Of healths five fathom deep; and then anon

Drums in his ear, at which he starts and wakes,

And being thus frighted, swears a prayer or two

And sleeps again. This is that very Mab

That plats the manes of horses in the night,

And bakes the elf-locks in foul sluttish hairs,

Which once untangled much misfortune bodes.

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Mock-epic = the literary mode applying epic vocabulary to absurdly small subjects; the Queen Mab speech treats fairy-machinery in epic rhetorical register.

• Asyndetic listing = list of items piled without conjunctions, producing accumulation.

• Stichomythia = rapid one-line exchanges between speakers, often signalling escalating conflict.

• Curse = a public verbal imprecation; Mercutio's "A plague o' both your houses" is the play's load-bearing curse.

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Mercutio as foil to Romeo — sceptical wit to lyric earnestness).

• Apostrophe = direct address to someone or something absent.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Mercutio's name derives from the Latin mercurialis — the volatile-moody temperament associated with the planet Mercury — and his Queen Mab speech in Act 1 Scene 4 was composed during the same 1594–95 lyric phase in which Shakespeare was writing the fairy-machinery of A Midsummer Night's Dream.

• Building sentence (historical context): That moment sat inside a wider 1590s English fascination with fairy-folklore, and Shakespeare's twin treatments of fairy material — Mercutio's compressed Mab and Oberon's expanded fairy-court — supplied the era with its two most virtuoso English fairy-set-pieces. Mercutio's speech is the play's lyric counter-voice to Romeo's earnest Petrarchanism, the same poetic moment producing two distinct registers.

• Thesis: Across Act 1 Scene 4's Queen Mab speech installed as verbal virtuosity, Act 3 Scene 1's wit weaponised against Tybalt and Romeo's interventions, and Act 3 Scene 1's dying "A plague o' both your houses" curse, Shakespeare stages Mercutio at three levels — virtuoso, satirist, and prophet — three stages of the same fast-moving wit progressing from fantasy to combat to prophecy across his short stage-life.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Act 1 Scene 4, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Act 1 Scene 4, the Queen Mab speech — verbal virtuoso installed)

• Topic: In the Act 1 Scene 4 Queen Mab speech, Shakespeare installs Mercutio at his first level — verbal virtuoso, the compressed-fantasy soliloquy demonstrating an imagination that builds and dismantles entire worlds in single sentences.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Mercutio's "She is the fairies' midwife, and she comes / In shape no bigger than an agate-stone / On the forefinger of an alderman, / Drawn with a team of little atomies / Over men's noses as they lie asleep" combines the asyndetic accumulation of miniaturising detail (agate-stone, alderman's finger, atomies — items piled in scaled descent) with the mock-epic register (epic-style descriptive enumeration applied to fairy-machinery).

• Close analysis: The agate-stone-on-an-alderman's-finger image scales the fairy-queen down to civic-jewellery dimensions; the noun "atomies" (the smallest particles imaginable) extends the miniaturisation to its final scale, the imagination contracting downward through three orders of size in three lines.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience recognises the rapid scaling and registers Mercutio's mind moving faster and finer than the play has yet permitted any other voice.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience detects the mock-epic register — epic vocabulary applied to fantastically small fairy-machinery — and infers the verbal virtuosity as Mercutio's signature.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be installing Mercutio's virtuosity at his first major appearance so that the death-scene of Act 3 Scene 1 registers as the silencing of the play's fastest-moving voice.

• Context (AO3): Mercutio's compressed Queen Mab and A Midsummer Night's Dream's expanded fairy-court were composed in the same 1594–95 lyric phase; Shakespeare's twin treatments of fairy material supplied the era with its two most virtuoso English fairy-set-pieces, and Mercutio's speech belongs to the same poetic moment as Oberon's later expansion.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Act 3 Scene 1, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 3 Scene 1, the wit-weaponised exchanges — satirist)

• Topic: Where Act 1 Scene 4 had installed verbal virtuosity, Act 3 Scene 1 converts it into wit weaponised — the same compressive imagination turned outward into pointed satire against Tybalt and the duelling-code.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Mercutio's exchanges with Tybalt — "Tybalt, you ratcatcher, will you walk?… What wouldst thou have with me?" — combine the dismissive metonymic insult "ratcatcher" (Tybalt's name Tybalt echoes a folk-name for cats, a pun the contemporary audience caught) with the rhetorical question "What wouldst thou have with me?" (the satirist's refusal to grant the duelling-code its required gravity).

• Close analysis: The metonymic "ratcatcher" trades on the Tybalt / Tybert-the-cat tradition from the medieval beast-fable Reynard the Fox, deflating the honour-system's gravity through a children's-story register; the rhetorical question refuses Tybalt's demand to be taken seriously on the duelling-code's own terms.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the same compressive virtuosity that built Queen Mab's machinery now turned against Tybalt, and registers wit functioning as combat-tool.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience absorbs the satire's target — the duelling-code's gravity itself — and infers Mercutio as the play's clearest critic of the system that will kill him.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing wit-as-weapon as the play's clearest counter-voice to honour-culture; Mercutio's satire is the social order's most articulate challenger inside the play's logic.

• Context (AO3): The medieval beast-fable Reynard the Fox, in which Tybert is the cat, was a folk-resource Elizabethan audiences shared; Mercutio's "ratcatcher" lands the satirical-deflationary register precisely because the audience recognises the children's-story origin of Tybalt's name.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Act 3 Scene 1, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Act 3 Scene 1, the dying curse — prophet)

• Topic: Where the wit had been weaponised against Tybalt, Mercutio's dying speech converts it to prophecy — "A plague o' both your houses" the play's most structurally important curse, prophetically naming the catastrophe before it arrives.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Mercutio's "A plague o' both your houses!" combines the curse-formula (a public verbal imprecation calling down disease on both households) with the parallelism "both your houses" (the curse refusing to take sides, calling the violence the houses share).

• Close analysis: The noun "plague" invokes a specific disease the contemporary audience recognised at first hand (London plague had closed the theatres 1592–94); the adjective "both" refuses to grant either house the relief of being the wronged party.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience picks up the curse and registers it as the play's prophetic voice — Mercutio at his death naming the structural catastrophe before it arrives.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience infers the parallelism "both your houses" and infers the curse as exactly the diagnosis the Prince had attempted in Act 1 — "airy word" converted into bodily prophecy.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing Mercutio's death-curse as the play's structural pivot — the verbal virtuoso whose final speech-act prophesies the catastrophe his own death has just made inevitable.

• Context (AO3): Emma Smith's This Is Shakespeare (Pelican, 2019) reads Act 3 Scene 1 as the play's decisive structural moment, and Mercutio's curse as the verbal articulation of the inevitability the social order has been producing across the play's first three acts.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Act 1 Scene 4 had installed as verbal virtuosity in the Queen Mab speech, Act 3 Scene 1 had weaponised as satire against Tybalt's duelling-code, and Act 3 Scene 1 had completed as prophecy in "A plague o' both your houses" — three stages of the same fast-moving wit progressing from fantasy to combat to prophecy across Mercutio's short stage-life.

• Controlling concept: The play argues that Verona's honour-culture makes the deaths of the young inevitable; Mercutio is the system's most articulate critic and its first major casualty, his curse the verbal articulation of the catastrophe the social order has been producing.

• Author's purpose: Shakespeare may be writing Mercutio as the play's verbal counter-voice to honour-culture — the satirical wit the system has no answer to, silenced by exactly the duelling-code his satire had been deflating.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that Mercutio's voice carries the play's clearest critique of the order that kills him, that his Queen Mab speech and his dying curse are the same wit operating at different scales, and that "A plague o' both your houses" is the prophecy the Prologue had announced and the Prince had warned, now spoken by the body the system has just consumed.

If a question arrives whose specific theme is not on this list, recombine — almost every AQA Romeo and Juliet theme can be answered with an Act 1 + an Act 3 + an Act 5 trio drawn from the rows above, plus one of the two critical voices below woven into a body-paragraph close.

Three context factors that travel across all 10 questions

These three context factors are the period-anchors most useful inside any act. Pick one per body paragraph, thread it briefly into the close analysis, and never bolt it on as a stand-alone sentence at the end of the essay.

1. Elizabethan honour-culture + dynastic marriage + Saviolo's duelling code. Most useful for: Q1, Q2, Q4, Q6, Q7, Q9 — any question on character / violence / feud / honour / marriage. Vincentio Saviolo's Practice (1595) codified the "lie circumstantial" code Tybalt invokes; Vives's Instruction of a Christian Woman (1529, reprinted through the 1590s) and Edmund Tilney's Flower of Friendship (1568) supplied conduct-literature affirming the consultative-father / dynastic-mechanics contradiction Capulet dramatises from Act 1 Scene 2 through Act 3 Scene 5; the Earl of Essex's 1590s court duels and Marlowe's 1593 killing supply the era's actual urban-violence anchor.

2. The 1592–94 London plague + plague-quarantine administration. Most useful for: Q5, Q8, Q4, Q10 — any question on fate / Friar Lawrence / violence / Mercutio. The bubonic outbreak of 1592–94 closed London's theatres for almost two years and institutionalised the "searchers of the town" role and the sealed-house quarantine policy the play references directly. Friar John's lost letter dramatises a real 1590s administrative failure; Mercutio's "A plague o' both your houses" invokes the disease the audience knew at first hand.

3. The Petrarchan tradition + Galenic medicine + the 1594–95 lyric phase. Most useful for: Q1, Q3, Q8, Q10 — any question on Romeo / love / Friar Lawrence / Mercutio. Petrarchan love-poetry, descended from the fourteenth-century Italian poet Petrarch, supplied two centuries of conventional oxymorons the play measures itself against; the Galenic medical tradition (Greek physician Galen, second century) supplied the era's herbal pharmacology — codified in John Gerard's Herball (London, 1597) — and the "lightning before death" clinical observation Romeo voices at the tomb. Shakespeare composed the play in his most prolific lyric phase, alongside the Sonnets and A Midsummer Night's Dream.

Two critical voices that travel across all 10 questions

Pick one critical voice per essay. Name the critic + their work + their year. Embed the critical voice inside a body-paragraph close, NOT as a stand-alone sentence in the conclusion.

1. Emma Smith, This Is Shakespeare (Pelican, 2019). Smith reads Romeo and Juliet as structurally inevitable — the deaths produced by Verona's social order rather than rescued from it by accident. The Prologue's announcement collapses suspense in advance; the play's interest lies in how the order produces the catastrophe, not whether. The lost letter is one of several mechanisms by which the social order delivers the announced ending. Apply to: any question about fate / love / character / Mercutio / Friar Lawrence / the feud — Smith's reading frames the catastrophe as systemic rather than accidental.

2. Coppélia Kahn, Man's Estate: Masculine Identity in Shakespeare (University of California Press, 1981). Kahn reads Shakespeare's tragedies through the masculine honour-system — the social order producing the deaths it claimed to defend against. Tybalt is the system's purest vehicle; Mercutio the system's most articulate critic; Romeo the young man whose lyric voice cannot escape the violence-script. Apply to: any question about violence / honour / dynasty / marriage / character — Kahn's reading frames the play as the era's clearest critique of masculine honour-culture producing exactly the catastrophes it claimed to prevent.

Reminder

• One context factor per body paragraph, threaded into the close, never bolted on at the end.

• One critical voice per essay, named with year, embedded inside a body-paragraph close.

• The controlling concept's three claims — Verona's honour-culture and dynastic-marriage system make the deaths of the young inevitable; the catastrophe is structurally produced rather than contingent on bad luck; adolescent love is the moral critique of the social order that destroys it — should land at least once across the introduction's thesis and the conclusion's restated thesis.

• The locked structure — Intro 3 / BP1-2-3 each 7 / Conclusion 4 — is non-negotiable. AQA examiners read for shape; the page must look like an essay.

• Whole-essay cohesion — BP2 opens "Where Act X had installed Y…", BP3 opens "Where Act Z had Y…", conclusion's restated thesis retraces the chain using the same key nouns.

• Sophicly content rules — honour and dynastic-marriage are the legitimate critical frames for Verona's social order; reframe any source material accordingly. Full text-specific rules in protocol §8.

Total target: ~1000 words on the page in the exam. Always under 1100. Always close on the controlling concept's final landing point — the deaths are produced by the social order rather than rescued from it by accident.
